div.div: Effect: A performer appears to eat glowing fire, placing burning material in their mouth. Method: tow (flax fiber) soaked in potassium nitrate smolders at a manageable temperature when the mouth's warm moisture controls the oxygen supply — closing the mouth extinguishes it. This is a genuine performance art requiring professional training, mentorship, and extensive practice. The original book itself warned it was 'not recommended for amateurs.' Never attempt without expert supervision. Modern fire performers train for months under mentors before their first live act.

Fire eating is one of the oldest performance arts in recorded history, practiced at ancient Roman circus events and throughout the medieval period by itinerant showmen. The fire eating scene in the 1862 book specifically notes it was 'not recommended for amateurs,' making it one of the earliest printed safety warnings in a magic book.

The performer appears to eat burning tow (flax fiber), placing glowing material in their mouth and quenching it. The method: tow soaked in niter (potassium nitrate) ignites easily in the presence of the mouth's warmth but can be extinguished safely by closing the mouth and excluding oxygen.

svg.text: Source: The Magician's Own Book (1862) · Heritage Parlor
